finding: The exact string 'Check /home/jonathan/repos/dotfiles for any uncommitted changes. If there are any, run: cd /home/jonathan/repos/dotfiles && git add -A && git commit...' appears verbatim in at least 5 sessions, pasted manually by the user. A cron-based sync agent already exists at /home/jonathan/Repos/dotfiles/sync-agent.sh running daily at 14:23. The manual prompts use the old lowercase 'repos' path (since corrected). The cron covers this task entirely, yet the user continued triggering it manually — possibly because the cron was set up mid-period, or because they wanted immediate sync rather than waiting for the daily schedule.
existing_mitigation: Cron job exists (sync-agent.sh at 14:23 daily) but runs only once per day, which may not satisfy the user's desire for immediate sync after making changes.

finding: The user plugged in their Kindle Scribe and Calibre could not detect it. This happened in two separate sessions. In the first session, the fix was applied but the user asked 'I want the fix to persist over reboots' — suggesting the initial attempt did not write a persistent udev rule. In the second session the same problem recurred, with the user noting 'I know you solved this problem for me before.' The fix is now persisted at /etc/udev/rules.d/51-kindle-scribe.rules. The pattern — a hardware workaround that needs to survive reboots but initially doesn't — is a common automation gap.
existing_mitigation: Fix now persisted. No further action likely needed unless the rule gets overwritten by system updates.

finding: Across three artcraft sessions, the user asks 'build/run project', 'git pull then build', and 'what is the command to build this project?' — each time needing Claude to figure out the build system. A simple build/run script or CLAUDE.md entry with the build command would eliminate this repeated discovery. The pattern is: user returns to a project after a gap, does not remember how to build it, and asks Claude to figure it out each time.

Autodoro runs as a systemd user service. After the user pushes code changes, the running service still uses the old code. In one session the user explicitly says 'push changes, and then reload the script in the service.' In another, the user had to ask 'why is autodoro not running?' because it had stopped after changes. A post-push hook or a CLAUDE.md rule to always restart the service after modifying autodoro code would make this automatic.
existing_mitigation: None found. The systemd service definition exists but there is no auto-reload mechanism tied to code changes.

finding: Across many sessions and projects, the user's final action is a manual request like 'commit and push', 'push changes', 'git commit dat shit', 'push it'. This is a concrete, recurring task with a consistent goal (persist and share work). The dotfiles sync-agent.sh already solves this for one repo. A generalized version — a Stop hook or CLAUDE.md rule that prompts Claude to offer commit+push when there are unstaged changes at session end — could reduce this friction across all projects.
existing_mitigation: dotfiles sync-agent.sh handles one repo via cron. No cross-project automation exists.
